The **2007 Saskatchewan general election** was held on November 7, 2007 to determine the composition of the [26th Legislative Assembly of Saskatchewan](/wiki/26th_Legislative_Assembly_of_Saskatchewan "26th Legislative Assembly of Saskatchewan").

The [Saskatchewan New Democratic Party](/wiki/New_Democratic_Party_of_Saskatchewan "New Democratic Party of Saskatchewan") government of [Premier](/wiki/Premier_of_Saskatchewan "Premier of Saskatchewan") [Lorne Calvert](/wiki/Lorne_Calvert "Lorne Calvert") was defeated by the [Saskatchewan Party](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Party "Saskatchewan Party"), led by [Brad Wall](/wiki/Brad_Wall "Brad Wall"). It was only the third time in the province's history that a [centre-right](/wiki/Conservatism_in_Canada "Conservatism in Canada") party had won power.

## Campaign

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2007_Saskatchewan_general_election&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Campaign")]

Lorne Calvert, Premier of Saskatchewan and leader of the [New Democratic Party](/wiki/New_Democratic_Party_of_Saskatchewan "New Democratic Party of Saskatchewan") (NDP), advised the [Lieutenant Governor of Saskatchewan](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_of_Saskatchewan "Lieutenant Governor of Saskatchewan") to call an election for November 7, 2007. In the [2003 election](/wiki/2003_Saskatchewan_general_election "2003 Saskatchewan general election"), the NDP won 30 of 58 seats to form a majority government. The [Saskatchewan Party](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Party "Saskatchewan Party"), then led by [Elwin Hermanson](/wiki/Elwin_Hermanson "Elwin Hermanson"), won 28 seats in that election.

Following that election, Hermanson resigned as leader, and [Swift Current](/wiki/Swift_Current_(provincial_electoral_district) "Swift Current (provincial electoral district)") MLA Brad Wall was [acclaimed as his successor](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Party_leadership_elections#2004_leadership_convention "Saskatchewan Party leadership elections") in March 2004.

Within a year after Wall took the leadership, the Saskatchewan Party unveiled a much more moderate policy platform in order to expand its support outside its rural base. This strategy paid off; by the spring of 2007 the Saskatchewan Party was well ahead in most opinion polling.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The NDP promised to create places for 10,000 new post-secondary students, and to lower tuition fees by $1,000. The Saskatchewan Party countered with a promise of a tuition rebate of up to $20,000 after graduation, spread over seven years.[[3]](#cite_note-voice-3)

At the leaders' debate, [Liberal](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Saskatchewan "Liberal Party of Saskatchewan") leader [David Karwacki](/wiki/David_Karwacki "David Karwacki") participated alongside Calvert and Wall despite the Liberals holding no seats going into the election. It is the last time to date that the leader of a party other than the NDP or the Saskatchewan Party has been invited to participate.

Five [New Democratic](/wiki/New_Democratic_Party_of_Saskatchewan "New Democratic Party of Saskatchewan") incumbents – [Graham Addley](/wiki/Graham_Addley "Graham Addley"), [Mark Wartman](/wiki/Mark_Wartman "Mark Wartman"), [Maynard Sonntag](/wiki/Maynard_Sonntag "Maynard Sonntag"), [Lon Borgerson](/wiki/Lon_Borgerson "Lon Borgerson") and [Glenn Hagel](/wiki/Glenn_Hagel "Glenn Hagel") – were defeated. Sonntag was initially declared elected in [Meadow Lake](/wiki/Meadow_Lake_(provincial_electoral_district) "Meadow Lake (provincial electoral district)") on election night, but was declared defeated the following day after a reported tabulation error. This result was confirmed following the counting of absentee ballots on November 19.

The other five seat gains for the [Saskatchewan Party](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Party "Saskatchewan Party") came in districts where the New Democratic incumbent did not stand for reelection.

This is the most recent election to date in which any party other than the NDP or the Saskatchewan Party received more than 5% of the vote.
